Jul.marine_life: lionfish and moray eels hunting the busy summer reefs while blue-spotted rays rest on the sand
Jul.conditions: very hot topside with the warmest, glassiest water

Aug.marine_life: warm-water reef life buzzing through the day while octopus and cuttlefish emerge at dusk
Aug.conditions: fierce heat on land with a bath-warm, flat sea

Sep.marine_life: eagle rays and turtles cruising through thick fish life while anthias clouds hover over the wrecks
Sep.conditions: cooling air, still-warm sea and visibility opening up

Oct.marine_life: turtles and rays weaving through thick reef fish while the macro life stays active
Oct.conditions: ideal temperatures above and below with thirty-metre visibility

Nov.marine_life: seahorses and frogfish showing themselves more readily while the reefs stay lively and crowds thin
Nov.conditions: comfortable air, gently cooling water and superb clarity

Dec.marine_life: resident reef life carrying on quietly while macro critters fill the uncrowded sites
Dec.conditions: cool clear water in a calm, sheltered gulf with mild winter days

destinations.highlights: Jordan's 27km Red Sea window packs the Cedar Pride wreck, a scuttled C-130 Hercules, a sunken tank and an underwater military museum alongside pristine coral gardens — nearly all divable straight off the beach.
destinations.currents: The Gulf of Aqaba is deeply sheltered, so currents are typically negligible to mild, with only a light longshore drift on some reef sites. This is one of the calmest stretches of the Red Sea and rarely dictates dive planning.
destinations.current_note: sheltered gulf, near-still water most days
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full suit May-Nov; 5mm (some add a hood) Dec-Apr when water dips to 21°C

dive_sites.name: Cedar Pride
dive_sites.blurb: A soft-coral-carpeted Lebanese freighter lying on her port side 150m off the beach — one of the Red Sea's most accessible signature wrecks.

dive_sites.name: C-130 Hercules
dive_sites.blurb: An intact four-engine cargo plane scuttled in 2017, sitting upright on sand with an easy cockpit-to-tail swim-through.

dive_sites.name: The Tank
dive_sites.blurb: An M42 anti-aircraft tank sunk just 20m from shore, now crusted in coral and a favourite for training dives and long safety-stop photo ops.

dive_sites.name: Japanese Garden
dive_sites.blurb: Aqaba's prettiest coral slope — table, brain and soft corals in botanical-garden density with turtles cruising the shallows.

dive_sites.name: Underwater Military Museum
dive_sites.blurb: A battle-formation column of scuttled tanks, troop carriers and a helicopter arranged down the reef slope since 2019.

dive_sites.name: Power Station
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off at the northern end of the coast with black coral, gorgonians and the best chance of pelagic fly-bys.

destinations.description: Jordan's single short coastline squeezes remarkable variety into the calm, current-free northern tip of the Red Sea: a legendary freighter wreck, a scuttled cargo plane, sunken military hardware and genuinely healthy fringing reefs, almost all reached by walking in from the beach. Water stays warm and flat year-round, visibility rarely drops below 20m, and macro hunting is superb. Ideal for new divers, wreck lovers and anyone pairing dives with Petra and Wadi Rum.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tiia Monto (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Aruba

Jan.marine_life: green and hawksbill turtles grazing reefs while barracuda shadow the wrecks and stingrays sift the sand
Jan.conditions: dry and sunny with steady trade winds and a protected west coast

Feb.marine_life: turtles and southern stingrays cruising past seahorses in the seagrass while reef fish crowd the Antilla
Feb.conditions: driest, coolest water with brisk trade winds and calm leeward sites

Mar.marine_life: eagle rays cruising the outer reefs while turtles roam wrecks carpeted in tube sponges and fish
Mar.conditions: height of the dry season with calm, clear west-coast water

Apr.marine_life: eagle rays and stingrays sweeping the sand while turtle numbers build toward nesting
Apr.conditions: sunny and settled with excellent clarity for the wrecks

May.marine_life: turtle nesting season opening with green and hawksbill encounters and barracuda schools offshore
May.conditions: warm and dry with strengthening trade winds and flat leeward sites

Jun.marine_life: turtles crowding every reef while rays cruise the sand flats and fish pack the Jane Sea
Jun.conditions: windiest stretch with surface chop though west-coast dives stay easy

Jul.marine_life: turtle hatchlings scrambling seaward past the adults while frogfish and seahorses reward sharp-eyed divers
Jul.conditions: strong trade winds with reliable sun, warm water and light chop

Aug.marine_life: turtles lingering while eagle rays patrol the outer reef and octopus hunt on night dives
Aug.conditions: hot and breezy, safely outside the hurricane belt with predictable seas

Sep.marine_life: big barracuda and rays riding the warmest water while juvenile fish swarm the wrecks
Sep.conditions: calmest trade winds with glassy mornings and long visibility

Oct.marine_life: rays and turtles holding their circuits while wreck life thrives and macro critters fill the seagrass
Oct.conditions: warmest sea of the year with brief showers and occasional swell

Nov.marine_life: turtles and stingrays making their year-round rounds while reef fish stay busy on the leeward reefs
Nov.conditions: wettest month with short rain bursts and slightly softer visibility

Dec.marine_life: turtles cruising the usual circuits while barracuda and eagle rays settle back over the reefs
Dec.conditions: tapering showers and rebuilding trade winds with easy holiday-season diving

destinations.highlights: The 120m Antilla — one of the Caribbean's largest diveable wrecks — headlines a leeward coast of WWII ships, sunken airplanes and shore-accessible reefs that dive well twelve months a year outside the hurricane belt.
destinations.currents: Constant northeast trade winds drive a west-flowing current along the island, so diving concentrates on the sheltered leeward coast where it is usually mild. Southern sites like the Jane Sea and Baby Beach Reef can run strong and are dived as drifts when it picks up.
destinations.current_note: mild on the leeward wrecks; can run strong at the southern reef sites
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit year-round; many divers go rash-guard only Aug-Oct

dive_sites.name: SS Antilla
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m German freighter scuttled in 1940 and one of the Caribbean's largest wrecks, shallow enough to cover in a single dive and blanketed in tube sponges and fish.

dive_sites.name: Pedernales
dive_sites.blurb: The scattered midsection of an oil tanker torpedoed by a U-boat in 1942, lying in calm shallows with cabins and pipeline sections to weave through.

dive_sites.name: Jane Sea
dive_sites.blurb: An upright 52m cement freighter sunk as an artificial reef in 1988, thickly overgrown with coral and swept by the currents that bring in barracuda and rays.

dive_sites.name: Sonesta Airplanes
dive_sites.blurb: A YS-11 turboprop and a deeper DC-3 sunk off Renaissance Island, giving Aruba its signature airplane-wreck double bill.

dive_sites.name: Mangel Halto
dive_sites.blurb: The island's best shore dive: a mangrove-fringed lagoon spilling onto a sloping reef where turtles are near-guaranteed and seahorses hide in the grass.

dive_sites.name: Baby Beach Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A hard-coral reef at the island's southeastern tip known for barracuda and turtles, with current that rewards a watchful drift.

destinations.description: Aruba sits at the Caribbean's southern edge, below the hurricane belt, where steady trade winds shape a dry, sunny island and a sheltered leeward coast that dives reliably all year. Wrecks are the story here: the vast Antilla, the torpedoed Pedernales, a coral-covered freighter and a pair of sunken airplanes, most in easy recreational depths. Warm water, short boat rides and genuine shore entries make it a natural pick for new wreck divers and mixed-ability groups.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Serge Melki from Indianapolis, USA (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Bali

Jan.marine_life: bumpheads grazing the Liberty at dawn while nudibranch numbers climb the muck slopes among wreck fish
Jan.conditions: wet-season runoff softening visibility though east-coast shore entries stay sheltered

Feb.marine_life: frogfish, ghost pipefish and octopus prowling the black sand while macro life thickens at Seraya
Feb.conditions: rainiest stretch with calm mornings and variable visibility after downpours

Mar.marine_life: turtles cruising the walls while reef fish spawning builds and harlequin shrimp and boxer crabs emerge
Mar.conditions: easing rains and settling seas with visibility recovering at Menjangan

Apr.marine_life: jacks and sweetlips crowding the Liberty while pygmy seahorses cling to fans and reef sharks patrol
Apr.conditions: early dry season with flat seas, warm water and fast-improving clarity

May.marine_life: bumphead parrotfish grazing the wreck at first light while garden eels sway under dense fish soup
May.conditions: glassy mornings with superb visibility along the east coast

Jun.marine_life: schools swirling over the wreck and walls while turtles cruise Menjangan and macro thrives at Seraya
Jun.conditions: dry, calm and clear everywhere with light southeast trade winds

Jul.marine_life: rhinopias and blue-ringed octopus emerging in the cooler water while critters multiply at Padang Bai
Jul.conditions: dry and clear with choppy boat crossings and noticeably cooler water

Aug.marine_life: reef sharks and schooling fish thriving in the cool upwellings while nudibranchs carpet the muck slopes
Aug.conditions: coolest water and briskest winds with strong visibility despite some swell

Sep.marine_life: fish life packing the Liberty while thermocline critters stay active and turtles hold steady at Menjangan
Sep.conditions: dry and reliable with easing winds and slowly warming water

Oct.marine_life: fish swarming the wreck and walls while gorgonian fans at Menjangan host pygmy seahorses
Oct.conditions: calm seas and the clearest water, especially over Menjangan's walls

Nov.marine_life: nudibranch numbers surging as plankton returns while the wreck and Blue Lagoon stay fishy and photogenic
Nov.conditions: exceptional clarity early in the month before the first rains

Dec.marine_life: mandarinfish and frogfish showing at the jetty while resident wreck fauna and macro life ramp up
Dec.conditions: building wet season with warm, sheltered shore diving and softer visibility

destinations.highlights: A coral-encrusted WWII wreck you can walk into off a pebble beach, world-class black-sand muck at Seraya and Padang Bai, and Menjangan's sheer gorgonian walls — all shore- or short-boat accessible year-round.
destinations.currents: The northeast coast at Tulamben and Amed is sheltered, with gentle currents that rarely trouble shore entries. Sites in the Padang Bai channel feel the Lombok Strait's pull and can switch on quickly, so guides time slack windows there.
destinations.current_note: calm shore sites; channel sites near Padang Bai can run
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full year-round; 5mm for the cooler Jul-Sep water and repetitive days

dive_sites.name: USAT Liberty, Tulamben
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m WWII cargo ship smothered in coral just 30m off the beach — arguably the world's easiest great wreck dive, with bumphead parrotfish at dawn.

dive_sites.name: Tulamben Drop-Off
dive_sites.blurb: A river-mouth wall plunging past big gorgonians and barrel sponges, with reef sharks and schooling fish in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Seraya Secrets
dive_sites.blurb: Bali's most famous black-sand macro slope, where photographers hunt harlequin shrimp, boxer crabs and ghost pipefish.

dive_sites.name: Blue Lagoon, Padang Bai
dive_sites.blurb: A calm white-sand bay packed with unusual critters — rhinopias, leaf scorpionfish and blue-ringed octopus turn up here.

dive_sites.name: Jemeluk Wall & Pyramids, Amed
dive_sites.blurb: An easy shore-entry wall and artificial-reef pyramids alive with glassfish, garden eels and passing turtles.

dive_sites.name: Menjangan Island
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer walls in a marine park off Bali's northwest tip, famous for the island's clearest water and gorgonian-draped drop-offs.

destinations.description: Bali's mainland coast packs remarkable variety into short taxi rides: the coral-carpeted USAT Liberty wreck at Tulamben, black-sand muck slopes at Seraya and Amed, critter-rich bays at Padang Bai, and Menjangan's crystalline walls in the northwest. Warm, mostly gentle water and shore entries make it a superb place to build skills, while the macro life keeps seasoned photographers busy for weeks. Dry-season months bring the flattest seas and clearest water.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tilonaut (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Cancún & Playa del Carmen

Jan.marine_life: bull sharks massing in numbers while sailfish ball baitfish offshore and eagle rays cruise the wrecks
Jan.conditions: dry season with coolest water and superb clarity between passing nortes

Feb.marine_life: pregnant bull sharks holding at Shark Point while the sailfish run rages and turtles drift past
Feb.conditions: dry, settled seas between fronts with excellent visibility

Mar.marine_life: last bull sharks lingering through mid-month while sailfish hunt offshore and reef fish and rays abound
Mar.conditions: dry and warming with calm seas and very good visibility

Apr.marine_life: turtles, eagle rays and nurse sharks patrolling the reefs while schools hang over the underwater sculptures
Apr.conditions: flat, warm and clear in a calm, uncrowded window

May.marine_life: first whale sharks arriving on the offshore banks mid-month while turtles begin nesting ashore
May.conditions: warm and mostly calm with sargassum on beaches but clean reefs

Jun.marine_life: whale shark numbers building offshore while turtles crowd the prolific reef life inshore
Jun.conditions: hot early rainy season with short afternoon storms and diveable seas

Jul.marine_life: hundreds of whale sharks aggregating offshore while turtles and rays cruise the reefs inshore
Jul.conditions: hot and humid with bathtub-warm water and calm mornings

Aug.marine_life: whale sharks swirling in a huge aggregation with mantas mixed in while turtles hold the reefs
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with calm spells between tropical squalls

Sep.marine_life: whale sharks dispersing mid-month while turtles, morays and barracuda carry the reef diving
Sep.conditions: height of hurricane season, diveable between systems with real cancellation risk

Oct.marine_life: rays and turtles riding the drifts over healthy reef life while pelagic action goes quiet
Oct.conditions: tail of hurricane season with easing rain and recovering visibility

Nov.marine_life: first bull sharks appearing off Playa del Carmen mid-month while eagle rays return to the wrecks
Nov.conditions: early dry season with the first nortes briefly stirring the sea

Dec.marine_life: bull sharks holding deep off Playa del Carmen while sailfish arrive offshore and eagle rays school
Dec.conditions: cooling, clear dry-season water with only the occasional norte

destinations.highlights: Two headline shark seasons — cageless bull shark dives at 24m off Playa del Carmen from November to March and the planet's biggest whale shark aggregation offshore in July-August — bolted onto easy drift reefs, hurricane-split wrecks and the sculpture galleries of the MUSA underwater museum on Manchones reef.
destinations.currents: The north-flowing Yucatán Current sweeps the coast, so most Playa del Carmen reef diving is done as relaxed one-way drifts with the boat following. Inshore Cancún sites like Manchones and MUSA sit in protected shallows with little water movement, while the deeper wrecks can carry genuinely strong flow.
destinations.current_note: Drift-style boat diving on the Playa reefs; strong current possible on the deep wrecks
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full year-round; 5mm comfortable for repetitive dives Dec-Mar

dive_sites.name: MUSA — Manchones
dive_sites.blurb: The underwater museum's main gallery, where hundreds of life-size sculptures on Manchones reef have become coral-crusted habitat in calm, clear shallows.

dive_sites.name: Manchones Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Long, shallow ridge of hard coral off Isla Mujeres with big schools of grunts and snapper, gentle conditions and generous bottom time.

dive_sites.name: Shark Point
dive_sites.blurb: A featureless sand flat off Playa del Carmen where kneeling divers watch pregnant bull sharks cruise within metres from November to March.

dive_sites.name: C-58 General Anaya
dive_sites.blurb: WWII-era minesweeper broken in two by hurricanes, opening swim-throughs that fill with eagle rays in winter; a live-drop drift dive in current.

dive_sites.name: Mama Viña
dive_sites.blurb: Shrimp trawler sunk as an artificial reef off Playa, sitting upright on sand and swept by current that keeps it dense with barracuda and sponges.

dive_sites.name: Tortugas
dive_sites.blurb: Playa's turtle alley — a mellow drift over sponge-covered slopes where green and loggerhead turtles feed at close range.

dive_sites.name: Los Arcos
dive_sites.blurb: Deeper Playa reef of arches, overhangs and swim-throughs that pulls in nurse sharks, rays and the occasional passing pelagic.

destinations.description: The Cancún–Playa del Carmen corridor is high-volume Caribbean diving with two genuinely wild seasons stitched in. Winter brings pregnant bull sharks to a 24-metre sand flat off Playa and sailfish to the blue; summer stacks hundreds of whale sharks offshore. Between them you drift warm, easy reefs on the Yucatán Current, land on hurricane-split wrecks, and tour the sculpture galleries of MUSA. Short boat rides and year-round warm water suit everyone from new divers to shark-focused veterans.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Safa in la  but  uploaded by Mardetanha (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Dubai & Fujairah
destinations.region: Gulf of Oman / United Arab Emirates

Jan.marine_life: turtles and blacktip reef sharks patrolling Dibba Rock while cuttlefish pair and morays guard the wrecks
Jan.conditions: cool, calm winter seas with the year's clearest water

Feb.marine_life: cuttlefish courting in the shallows while eagle rays glide past jawfish colonies and resident turtles
Feb.conditions: coolest water of the year yet settled and clear

Mar.marine_life: rays and guitarfish gliding over the sand while nudibranchs bloom on Martini Rock and turtles abound
Mar.conditions: warming and mostly calm with occasional plankton haze

Apr.marine_life: baitballs building offshore while barracuda schools circle and blacktips patrol the shallows
Apr.conditions: warm and pleasant though spring blooms can soften visibility

May.marine_life: first whale shark reports around Dibba Rock while snapper aggregations build and batfish hover nearby
May.conditions: getting hot topside with a friendly sea and variable visibility

Jun.marine_life: whale sharks arriving in earnest while plankton feeders gather and turtles shrug off the heat
Jun.conditions: bathwater sea with hazy plankton-rich water and hot boat rides

Jul.marine_life: whale sharks cruising reliably while big schools of fusiliers and jacks fill the green water
Jul.conditions: sea above thirty degrees with milky visibility and extreme surface heat

Aug.marine_life: whale sharks still cruising through while reef life lies subdued in the warmest, greenest water
Aug.conditions: fiercest heat with likely algal blooms and visibility that can collapse

Sep.marine_life: late whale sharks passing through while barracuda tornadoes return and morays and scorpionfish crowd the wrecks
Sep.conditions: still hot with lingering bloom risk as the sea settles

Oct.marine_life: rays returning over the sand while snapper and trevally school and turtles crowd Snoopy Island
Oct.conditions: cooling and clearing as comfortable diving resumes

Nov.marine_life: blacktips patrolling while honeycomb morays gape, cuttlefish arrive and dense fish life smothers Martini Rock
Nov.conditions: calm, clear and comfortable as the sweet spot begins

Dec.marine_life: turtles and eagle rays cruising the coral outcrops while cuttlefish hover over prolific macro life
Dec.conditions: cool, glassy winter sea with the clearest water

destinations.highlights: Easy year-round diving on the Gulf of Oman east coast — turtle-packed reefs at Dibba Rock and Snoopy Island, soft-coral-crusted Martini Rock, the purpose-sunk Inchcape wrecks, plus Dubai's artificial-reef wreck sites, with summer whale shark visits.
destinations.currents: Currents on the east coast are generally mild, with only occasional gentle drift along exposed outcrops like Martini Rock and the Inchcape wrecks. Surge can pick up in the shallows when swell wraps around Dibba Rock.
destinations.current_note: mostly slack; light current or surge on exposed sites
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full Oct-May (5mm midwinter for the cold-prone); shorty or 1mm skin in 31-32°C summer water

dive_sites.name: Dibba Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A protected rocky islet whose sloping reef delivers turtles on nearly every dive, patrolling blacktips and the coast's most reliable whale shark encounters in summer.

dive_sites.name: Snoopy Island
dive_sites.blurb: A shore-accessible boulder islet off Al Aqah where hard-coral gardens shelter turtles, rays and dense schooling fish in easy, shallow water.

dive_sites.name: Martini Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A submerged pinnacle carpeted in orange teddy-bear soft coral, famous for macro life, nudibranchs, morays and winter cuttlefish.

dive_sites.name: Inchcape 1
dive_sites.blurb: A 21m supply vessel purpose-sunk in 2001, sitting upright at 30m and now smothered in growth with lionfish, jacks and big morays.

dive_sites.name: Inchcape 2
dive_sites.blurb: The shallower sister wreck near Martini Wall, sunk in 2002 as an accessible artificial reef busy with batfish, snapper and scorpionfish.

dive_sites.name: Car Cemetery
dive_sites.blurb: Rows of sunken vehicles on the Fujairah sand that have grown into a quirky artificial reef stacked with morays, rays and glassfish.

dive_sites.name: Zainab Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: Dubai's signature wreck — a 70m smuggling tanker scuttled in 2001, lying on her port side and swarmed by barracuda, snapper and batfish.

destinations.description: The UAE's real diving lives on the Fujairah side, where the Gulf of Oman's plankton-rich water feeds rocky reefs at Dibba Rock, Snoopy Island and Martini Rock, plus the purpose-sunk Inchcape wrecks; Dubai adds sandy-bottom wreck sites and artificial reefs in the Gulf. Expect warm, easy conditions with modest visibility that peaks in winter and can green out during summer blooms — when whale sharks arrive. Ideal for new divers, wreck fans and layover diving.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Wongleejean (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Florida Keys

Jan.marine_life: turtles and nurse sharks resting among thick reef fish while sailfish slash bait balls offshore
Jan.conditions: windy, choppy cold fronts with superb clarity between them

Feb.marine_life: grunts and snapper schooling thick over the reefs while spotted eagle rays cruise the reef edge
Feb.conditions: coolest water with occasional front-driven blowouts and crystal-clear calm days

Mar.marine_life: reef life waking up while eagle rays and turtles cruise past big barracuda on the wrecks
Mar.conditions: tapering fronts and settling seas with still-cool water

Apr.marine_life: turtles nesting on the beaches while reef fish spawning activity builds across the reefs
Apr.conditions: calming seas, warming water and reliable boat schedules

May.marine_life: angelfish and parrotfish busying the reefs while Caribbean reef sharks patrol the deep wrecks
May.conditions: flat calm stretches with warm water and long daylight

Jun.marine_life: tarpon and permit stacking on the wrecks while nurse sharks and turtles turn up everywhere
Jun.conditions: warm and mostly calm with low early-season hurricane risk

Jul.marine_life: lobster marching across the reef while big jacks swirl over the wrecks
Jul.conditions: bathwater-warm with glassy mornings and the year's busiest boats

Aug.marine_life: goliath grouper aggregating on the deep wrecks while coral spawning follows the full moon
Aug.conditions: hot and calm between systems with storms brewing in the tropics

Sep.marine_life: the goliath grouper aggregation filling the Spiegel Grove and Vandenberg with dozens of two-hundred-kilogram fish
Sep.conditions: statistical hurricane peak yet superb diving when the weather holds

Oct.marine_life: lingering goliaths holding the wrecks while thick baitfish clouds swirl and reef sharks patrol
Oct.conditions: fading storm risk with warm water and thinning crowds

Nov.marine_life: reef fish schooling densely while eagle rays return and the wrecks stay quiet and fishy
Nov.conditions: first mild fronts arriving, mostly diveable with clearing visibility

Dec.marine_life: turtles and nurse sharks resting among schooling grunts while occasional pelagics pass offshore
Dec.conditions: returning cold fronts with windy spells and clear, cooler water between

destinations.highlights: America's reef tract in one island chain — the towering Spiegel Grove and Vandenberg wrecks, the Christ of the Abyss statue, spur-and-groove gardens at Molasses Reef and Looe Key, and a late-summer goliath grouper aggregation that turns the deep wrecks into cathedrals of giants.
destinations.currents: The Gulf Stream runs just offshore, washing the outer reefs with clear water and pushing moderate — occasionally strong — current across the deep wrecks like the Duane and Spiegel Grove. Inshore spur-and-groove reefs are usually gentle.
destinations.current_note: Deep wrecks can rip when the Gulf Stream swings in; descend on the mooring line
destinations.wetsuit: Shorty or 3mm in summer; 5mm full suit December-March

dive_sites.name: Spiegel Grove
dive_sites.blurb: A 155m landing ship dock off Key Largo — one of the world's largest artificial reefs, sprawling enough for a dozen dives and a goliath grouper magnet in late summer.

dive_sites.name: Christ of the Abyss
dive_sites.blurb: A bronze statue standing arms-raised in the shallows of Key Largo Dry Rocks, ringed by coral heads and clouds of grunts — the Keys' most photographed dive.

dive_sites.name: Molasses Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Gulf Stream-washed spur-and-groove with the best visibility in the Upper Keys, huge snapper schools and the old Slobodna windlass.

dive_sites.name: USNS Vandenberg
dive_sites.blurb: A 159m missile-tracking ship off Key West whose giant radar dishes make it one of the most photogenic wrecks in the Atlantic.

dive_sites.name: Looe Key
dive_sites.blurb: The Lower Keys' classic spur-and-groove sanctuary reef, dense with elkhorn relics, parrotfish and resident goliath grouper.

dive_sites.name: USCGC Duane
dive_sites.blurb: An upright, fully intact coast guard cutter sitting in the Gulf Stream's path — current-swept, sharky and blue.

destinations.description: The Florida Keys string 200km of the continental US's only coral barrier reef alongside a fleet of massive purpose-sunk warships. Days here alternate easy spur-and-groove reef dives at Molasses and Looe Key with serious deep wrecks — Spiegel Grove, Duane, Vandenberg — and in August and September goliath grouper aggregate on those hulls by the dozen. Easy logistics and warm water suit new divers; the wrecks reward advanced tickets. Hurricane season demands flexible plans.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tane Casserley/National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Koh Tao

Jan.marine_life: reef fish rebounding after the monsoon while barracuda schools return to the pinnacles
Jan.conditions: easing northeast monsoon with calming seas and slowly recovering visibility

Feb.marine_life: chevron barracuda and trevally schooling back in numbers while healthy macro fills the bays
Feb.conditions: often glassy, mirror-calm seas with clear water and light winds

Mar.marine_life: whale sharks arriving at Chumphon Pinnacle while plankton blooms pull them in from the blue
Mar.conditions: hot, dry and flat calm with superb visibility on the pinnacles

Apr.marine_life: whale sharks cruising through at their most reliable while giant trevally hunt fusilier balls
Apr.conditions: hottest, calmest stretch of the year with the best visibility

May.marine_life: whale sharks still passing through while big schools of batfish and barracuda circle the pinnacles
May.conditions: warm and mostly calm with the first southwest-monsoon breezes late

Jun.marine_life: groupers and snapper holding the pinnacle life steady while triggerfish nesting kicks off in the shallows
Jun.conditions: occasional southwest-monsoon chop while east-coast sites stay sheltered

Jul.marine_life: fusilier schools swirling thick at Chumphon while barracuda stack over Southwest Pinnacle
Jul.conditions: short showers and some wind-driven waves with variable but often fine visibility

Aug.marine_life: trevally hunting through dense baitfish while the occasional whale shark cruises past
Aug.conditions: often surprisingly calm and clear despite the season

Sep.marine_life: whale-shark sightings picking back up while the pinnacles hit their fishiest stretch
Sep.conditions: calm seas and clear water before the northeast monsoon builds

Oct.marine_life: whale sharks still turning up early in the month while schooling fish remain thick
Oct.conditions: transitional weather with increasing showers and the first monsoon swells

Nov.marine_life: reef life sheltering in the bays while runoff-clouded water keeps sightings limited
Nov.conditions: heavy northeast-monsoon rain and wind with the year's worst visibility

Dec.marine_life: hardy reef residents toughing it out while big animals stay scarce in the stirred-up water
Dec.conditions: strong winds and frequent downpours with weather-dependent boats and reduced visibility

destinations.highlights: Granite pinnacles rising from the Gulf of Thailand where whale sharks cruise through walls of barracuda, ringed by calm, shallow bays that make some of the easiest learning conditions anywhere.
destinations.currents: The sheltered bays around the island are nearly current-free, which is why so many divers learn here. The offshore pinnacles — Chumphon, Southwest and Sail Rock — can carry moderate current that concentrates the schooling fish.
destinations.current_note: calm bays; moderate flow possible on the offshore pinnacles
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or rash guard year-round; 3mm full if you run cold in Dec-Feb

dive_sites.name: Chumphon Pinnacle
dive_sites.blurb: The Gulf's marquee site — an anemone-carpeted granite tower swarmed by trevally and barracuda, and the island's most reliable whale-shark spot.

dive_sites.name: Sail Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A lone rock between Koh Tao and Koh Phangan with a vertical chimney swim-through and frequent whale-shark visits.

dive_sites.name: Southwest Pinnacle
dive_sites.blurb: A cluster of anemone-topped peaks patrolled by big groupers and dense barracuda schools.

dive_sites.name: HTMS Sattakut
dive_sites.blurb: A 48m former navy vessel sunk as an artificial reef, its gun turrets now home to groupers and glassfish.

dive_sites.name: White Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A sprawling two-part reef busy with angelfish and territorial titan triggerfish — the island's classic night dive.

dive_sites.name: Twins
dive_sites.blurb: Two boulder clusters in easy shallows, famous for a resident family of saddleback anemonefish.

destinations.description: Koh Tao is where the Gulf of Thailand earns its reputation as an easy, generous place to dive: warm, calm bays over shallow reef for new divers, and offshore granite pinnacles that punch far above their weight. Chumphon Pinnacle and Sail Rock stack barracuda, trevally and — in spring and early autumn — whale sharks over anemone-covered rock. Budget-friendly and boat rides are short; skip the November-December monsoon and it delivers year-round.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: User: (WT-shared) Whatsinaname at  wts wikivoyage (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Mallorca

Jan.marine_life: grouper, octopus and moray holding tight on quiet reefs while scorpionfish lie camouflaged
Jan.conditions: cold water and winter swells with many operators closed but clear visibility

Feb.marine_life: grouper, conger and lobster sheltering deep in the caves while nudibranchs begin appearing
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with weather-dependent boat days

Mar.marine_life: nudibranchs carpeting the reef and octopus roaming while the first cuttlefish move inshore
Mar.conditions: still cold with mixed seas as the winter fronts fade

Apr.marine_life: cuttlefish and octopus mating inshore while grouper emerge from their winter lies
Apr.conditions: seas settling and water slowly warming as dive centres reopen

May.marine_life: barracuda schools regrouping over El Toro while dusky grouper grow bold in the reserves
May.conditions: calm spells lengthening with warming water and clear pre-season visibility

Jun.marine_life: barracuda tornadoes swirling and eagle rays passing while grouper patrol the busy cave life
Jun.conditions: warm flat summer seas beginning with long daylight and excellent visibility

Jul.marine_life: barracuda and bream schooling densely while tame dusky grouper, octopus and moray work every dive
Jul.conditions: warmest calmest seas of the year with light beaming into the caverns

Aug.marine_life: barracuda walling up at El Toro and Malgrats with grouper, eagle ray and the odd amberjack
Aug.conditions: warmest water and thirty-metre-plus visibility but the busiest boats and sites

Sep.marine_life: amberjack hunting hard while barracuda, grouper and rays crowd reefs at their richest
Sep.conditions: sea still warm and crowds thinning, generally settled before late autumn storms

Oct.marine_life: barracuda and grouper still thick in the reserves as cuttlefish return
Oct.conditions: pleasantly warm water and quieter sites with occasional storm days

Nov.marine_life: reef life settling into winter mode with grouper, moray and lobster tucked in the overhangs
Nov.conditions: cooling fast with more wind and swell as many centres wind down

Dec.marine_life: grouper, conger and octopus holding on as hardy residents for those who brave it
Dec.conditions: cold water and narrow winter weather windows with limited operator schedules

destinations.highlights: Sunlit Mediterranean caverns, swim-throughs and air-dome caves paired with the barracuda schools and tame dusky grouper of the El Toro and Malgrats marine reserves.
destinations.currents: The Balearics are largely tide-free, so most sites see little to no current. Exposed headlands and the offshore reserve islets can pick up mild surface current or surge when the wind has been blowing.
destinations.current_note: mostly still water; light surge at exposed islets
destinations.wetsuit: 3-5mm full suit Jun-Oct; 7mm semi-dry or drysuit Nov-May

dive_sites.name: El Toro Marine Reserve
dive_sites.blurb: Protected islet where dense barracuda schools and fearless dusky grouper patrol a sloping reef that drops past 35m for conger and john dory.

dive_sites.name: Malgrats Islands
dive_sites.blurb: Twin-islet marine reserve whose wall, tunnels and arches hold midwater barracuda, big bream, grouper and lobster.

dive_sites.name: The Cathedral
dive_sites.blurb: Vast cavern with a breathable air dome, sheltering moray, scorpionfish, crawfish and octopus in its shadows.

dive_sites.name: El Formatge (The Cheese)
dive_sites.blurb: Hole-riddled rock islet off Cala Ratjada laced with swim-through tunnels frequented by octopus, moray and nudibranchs.

dive_sites.name: Sa Dragonera
dive_sites.blurb: Nature-park island off the west coast with clear-water walls, caves and passing pelagics.

dive_sites.name: Cave of Light (Portals Vells)
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow cavern famous for the shafts of sunlight that cut through its blue entrance on calm summer days.

destinations.description: Mallorca is the Mediterranean's limestone playground: decades of erosion have drilled its coastline into caverns, arches and swim-throughs, most of them shallow, sunlit and forgiving. The El Toro and Malgrats marine reserves supply the fish — wall-to-wall barracuda and dusky grouper so protected they ignore you. With tide-free seas, easy day-boat logistics and 30-metre summer visibility, it suits new divers building skills and cavern fans alike; May to October is the season that matters.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Strubbl (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Mauritius
destinations.difficulty: beginner-friendly, with advanced wrecks and caverns

Jan.marine_life: turtles, moray eels and reef sharks working the west coast while warm-water reef life stays busy
Jan.conditions: hot humid weather with cyclone risk building and calm spells between systems

Feb.marine_life: reef fish and turtles still about while swell and runoff scatter the action
Feb.conditions: cyclone storms can blow out diving for days and stir the lagoons

Mar.marine_life: frogfish and morays holding on the wrecks while the year's warmest water keeps reefs lively
Mar.conditions: cyclone risk tapering but still real, settled weeks diving very well

Apr.marine_life: turtles and barracuda spreading out while grey reef sharks patrol the Cathedral drop-off
Apr.conditions: cyclone season over with light winds, settled seas and clearest water

May.marine_life: dogtooth tuna and pelagics starting to cruise the north as big schools build
May.conditions: calm dry early-winter weather with comfortable seas on the sheltered west coast

Jun.marine_life: pelagic tuna and trevally massing in dense fish shoals as first humpback whales arrive offshore
Jun.conditions: cooler and breezier as trade winds build, west-coast sites stay protected

Jul.marine_life: humpback whales migrating past while shoaling fish, spinner dolphins and macro critters work the reefs
Jul.conditions: coolest water and steady southeast trades, calmest on west-coast mornings

Aug.marine_life: humpback activity building offshore while reef life stays steady with morays, scorpionfish and nudibranchs
Aug.conditions: windiest month with swell on exposed coasts, sheltered west-coast diving some days

Sep.marine_life: whales still passing while big schools linger and turtles stay reliable on the wrecks
Sep.conditions: winds beginning to ease with cool clear water and improving sea states

Oct.marine_life: sharks patrolling Coin de Mire while turtles and dense wreck life wake with warming water
Oct.conditions: dry settled shoulder season with light winds, flat seas and excellent visibility

Nov.marine_life: reef sharks, barracuda and rays working thick reef-fish aggregations across the sites
Nov.conditions: warm calm clear water before the wet season, the year's best conditions

Dec.marine_life: turtles, morays and pelagics circling the northern islets as summer abundance fills the reefs
Dec.conditions: warm and mostly settled early, humidity and showers rising toward New Year

destinations.highlights: A volcanic island ringed by lagoon and drop-off, pairing the cavern-lit Cathedral at Flic en Flac with fish-thick wrecks like the Stella Maru and Djabeda and shark-patrolled walls off Coin de Mire islet.
destinations.currents: Most lagoon-side and west-coast sites are calm, but exposed reefs like Rempart Serpent and the passes around Coin de Mire can run moderate to strong with the tide. Boats time slack water on the wrecks, so currents rarely dictate the day.
destinations.current_note: gentle inside the reef, brisker on exposed northern passes
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full in summer (Nov-Apr); 5mm in winter (Jun-Sep) when water dips to 23°C

dive_sites.name: Cathedral
dive_sites.blurb: Flic en Flac's signature dive — a shaft of light pours into a boulder-roofed chamber on the drop-off where grey reef sharks and soldierfish shelter.

dive_sites.name: Stella Maru
dive_sites.blurb: A trawler scuttled off Trou aux Biches decades ago, now a coral-dressed artificial reef with frogfish, porcupinefish and a resident giant moray.

dive_sites.name: Djabeda
dive_sites.blurb: A 44m Japanese fishing vessel sitting upright off Coin de Mire since 1998, wrapped in soft coral and circled by tuna and honeycomb morays.

dive_sites.name: The Wall, Coin de Mire
dive_sites.blurb: A sheer face off the northern islet where clear oceanic water brings sharks, barracuda and passing pelagics close to the rock.

dive_sites.name: Rempart Serpent
dive_sites.blurb: A snake-shaped ridge off Flic en Flac famous for an improbable density of scorpionfish, stonefish and moray eels on one small reef.

dive_sites.name: Blue Bay Marine Park
dive_sites.blurb: A protected southeastern lagoon and outer reef with healthy hard coral gardens — the island's gentlest introduction to its underwater life.

destinations.description: Mauritius wraps a volcanic island in barrier reef, so most diving happens minutes offshore where the lagoon wall drops into blue water. The west coast around Flic en Flac delivers the light-shafted Cathedral cavern and macro-rich ridges, while the north stacks wrecks and shark walls around Coin de Mire islet. It suits divers who want variety — caverns, scuttled ships, coral gardens — without long crossings, though January-to-March cyclones can interrupt; October to December and April to May dive best.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: "dronepicr" (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Muscat & Daymaniyat Islands

Jan.marine_life: leopard sharks resting on the sand with turtles, eagle rays and dense reef fish
Jan.conditions: coolest water with thermoclines, mostly calm seas and clear winter visibility

Feb.marine_life: leopard sharks cruising alongside honeycomb morays, rays and nudibranchs at their best
Feb.conditions: chilly thermoclines below fifteen metres with settled seas and good clarity

Mar.marine_life: turtles gathering while leopard sharks hold and mobula rays start cruising through
Mar.conditions: warming water and calm seas with visibility improving toward its spring best

Apr.marine_life: mobula squadrons sweeping through while green and hawksbill turtles and big snapper schools gather
Apr.conditions: flat seas with some of the clearest water of the year

May.marine_life: turtles nesting on the islands while mobulas, cuttlefish and dense fish soup fill the water
May.conditions: warm and calm with island landings closed but diving carrying on

Jun.marine_life: plankton drawing in rays and the first big pelagic visitors
Jun.conditions: very warm surface water with blooms starting to green the visibility

Jul.marine_life: early whale shark chances with mobulas herding baitballs through green water
Jul.conditions: high heat and plankton with visibility dropping but seas staying diveable

Aug.marine_life: whale sharks feeding on the blooms while devil rays and huge trevally schools sweep past
Aug.conditions: hot and humid with milky plankton-rich water and occasional swell

Sep.marine_life: whale shark aggregations building, sometimes several per dive, with mobulas alongside
Sep.conditions: blooms easing and visibility rebuilding over warm calm seas

Oct.marine_life: late whale sharks and mobulas lingering while turtles and leopard sharks return to the reef
Oct.conditions: clearing fast with the sweet spot of big animals and good visibility

Nov.marine_life: mobula rays and eagle rays cruising while turtles crowd the thick reef life everywhere
Nov.conditions: clear blue water with comfortable temperatures and reliably calm days

Dec.marine_life: leopard sharks and turtles patrolling while morays, seahorses and macro fill the reefs
Dec.conditions: cooling water and first thermoclines with clean winter visibility

destinations.highlights: A protected island nature reserve an hour off Muscat where whale sharks gather over pristine hard coral in September-October, leopard sharks and turtles laze on the sand year-round, and an intact 84m navy wreck waits in a sheltered bay nearby.
destinations.currents: Currents around the Daymaniyats are generally gentle, with occasional moderate flow along exposed walls and over the Al Munassir wreck. Most sites are relaxed plateau and bay dives suited to easy multilevel profiles.
destinations.current_note: mostly mild; occasional pushes on walls and the wreck
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full May-Oct; 5mm Nov-Apr for winter thermoclines

dive_sites.name: Aquarium
dive_sites.blurb: An oval coral plateau dropping to 30m, packed with leopard sharks, honeycomb morays, turtles and clouds of snapper.

dive_sites.name: Police Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A drop-off on Police Island where whale sharks and mobulas cruise past divers in season.

dive_sites.name: Three Sisters
dive_sites.blurb: Boulder swim-throughs draped in soft tree corals, reliable for eagle rays, leopard sharks and both turtle species.

dive_sites.name: Al Munassir Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An 84m navy landing ship scuttled in 2003, sitting upright and intact with guns in place, penetrable and thick with morays.

dive_sites.name: Fahal Island
dive_sites.blurb: Muscat's 'shark island' with a swim-through cavern, blacktip reef sharks, rays and passing dolphins.

dive_sites.name: Bandar Khayran
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered fjord-like bays of coral gardens ideal for easy dives, night dives and macro hunting.

destinations.description: An hour's boat ride from Oman's capital, the Daymaniyat Islands are a strictly protected reserve where Gulf of Oman nutrients meet genuinely healthy hard coral. Winter brings clear water, leopard sharks and turtles; summer plankton blooms trade visibility for whale sharks that peak in September and October, with mobula squadrons either side. Add Fahal Island and the sheltered wreck-filled bays of Bandar Khayran, and it suits any diver who values animals over infinity-pool viz.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: "dronepicr" (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Oahu

Jan.marine_life: humpback whale song filling dives while green turtles, whitetip reef sharks and eagle rays patrol wrecks
Jan.conditions: big winter swell shuts the North Shore, south-shore boat diving holds up

Feb.marine_life: humpbacks singing underwater while turtles hold at cleaning stations and spinner dolphins cruise the west side
Feb.conditions: coolest water with large north swells, sheltered south and west shores diveable

Mar.marine_life: last humpbacks leaving while Galapagos and sandbar sharks work offshore, octopus and frogfish on the reefs
Mar.conditions: north swell easing late month with passing fronts stirring shallow sites

Apr.marine_life: turtles and eagle rays cruising while schooling bluestripe snapper pack the YO-257 and Sea Tiger
Apr.conditions: swell windows opening with seas settling, water warming and visibility improving

May.marine_life: green turtles roaming while spinner dolphin pods, endemic reef fish and whitetips fill the lava tubes
May.conditions: the North Shore reopening with calm seas island-wide and clean blue water

Jun.marine_life: turtles and spinner dolphins about while Galapagos sharks show on offshore trips amid dense reef-fish activity
Jun.conditions: flat summer seas, steady trade winds, excellent visibility on all coasts

Jul.marine_life: turtles, octopus and eels filling the Sharks Cove lava tubes while tiger sharks patrol offshore banks
Jul.conditions: warmest and calmest stretch with long flat spells and twenty-five-metre-plus visibility

Aug.marine_life: green turtles queuing at cleaning stations while spotted eagle rays and spinner dolphins cruise Kahe Point
Aug.conditions: warm water and gentle seas with all four shores routinely diveable

Sep.marine_life: fish massing while turtles, reef sharks and rays work the wrecks with an occasional monk seal
Sep.conditions: often the calmest and clearest stretch of the year across the island

Oct.marine_life: turtles and eagle rays holding the Waikiki wrecks, Galapagos sharks offshore, spinner dolphins westside
Oct.conditions: first north swells arriving late month, south shore stays reliable

Nov.marine_life: first humpbacks arriving while turtles, whitetips and big schools gather on the south-shore wrecks
Nov.conditions: the North Shore closes as surf builds, Waikiki boat diving stays consistent

Dec.marine_life: humpback whales returning in numbers while turtles hold inshore and sandbar and Galapagos sharks patrol offshore
Dec.conditions: winter swell and rain squalls confine diving to south and west shores

destinations.highlights: Two intact wrecks minutes off Waikiki, summer lava-tube diving on the North Shore, green turtles at every cleaning station and pelagic sharks on the offshore banks.
destinations.currents: Most reef sites see mild tidal flow, but the offshore wrecks off Waikiki can carry noticeable current on the mooring lines, and winter swell adds strong surge to shallow shore sites. Channels between coasts and the offshore shark banks run stronger.
destinations.current_note: mild on reefs; current and surge on wrecks and in winter

dive_sites.name: YO-257 & San Pedro
dive_sites.blurb: Twin wrecks off Waikiki sunk as artificial reefs, swarming with turtles, eagle rays and schooling snapper a short boat ride from Honolulu.

dive_sites.name: Sea Tiger
dive_sites.blurb: Largely intact former trading vessel with open swim-throughs, resident turtles and whitetips at the edge of recreational depth.

dive_sites.name: Sharks Cove
dive_sites.blurb: North Shore lava tubes and caverns that only open in summer flat spells, packed with turtles, octopus and endemic reef fish.

dive_sites.name: Corsair
dive_sites.blurb: A WWII-era fighter plane sitting upright on a bare sand flat, ringed by garden eels and occasional passing eagle rays.

dive_sites.name: Kahe Point (Electric Beach)
dive_sites.blurb: Warm-water outflow on the west side that draws crowds of fish, green turtles and regular spinner dolphin fly-bys.

dive_sites.name: Makaha Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Collapsed lava-tube network on the leeward coast with skylights, resting whitetip sharks and reliably easy conditions.

dive_sites.name: Hanauma Bay Witches Brew
dive_sites.blurb: Outer corner of the protected crater bay where dense, unafraid fish life and turtle traffic reward a longer swim.

destinations.description: Oahu packs real variety into one very reachable island: intact wrecks like the YO-257 and Sea Tiger minutes off Waikiki, lava-tube caverns at Sharks Cove and Makaha, and shark banks offshore where Galapagos and tiger sharks patrol. Green turtles are near-guaranteed, spinner dolphins escort west-side boats, and winter dives come with humpback song. South and west shores dive year-round; the North Shore is a summer-only prize when the surf sleeps.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: nick hoke (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Phuket

Jan.marine_life: leopard sharks lying at Shark Point while mantas show at Racha Noi over dense reef fish
Jan.conditions: dry-season calm with flat crossings to all local sites and excellent visibility

Feb.marine_life: mantas cruising Racha Noi's south tip while leopard and blacktip reef sharks and barracuda schools patrol
Feb.conditions: calmest seas of the year with light winds and superb visibility

Mar.marine_life: manta chances continuing with turtles and leopard sharks while plankton pulses pull in big filter feeders
Mar.conditions: warm glassy seas with visibility occasionally hazy from plankton

Apr.marine_life: mantas making their last reliable Racha Noi window while leopard sharks, snapper and fusilier schools gather
Apr.conditions: hottest month with seas still mostly calm before the monsoon turns

May.marine_life: leopard sharks and turtles working moray-rich pinnacles as the reef life carries on unchanged
May.conditions: southwest monsoon arriving with passing squalls and crossings getting lumpier

Jun.marine_life: bamboo sharks holding the King Cruiser with healthy reef action and macro across the Rachas
Jun.conditions: green season with boats favouring sheltered east-coast Racha sites and variable visibility

Jul.marine_life: fish packing Racha Yai's east bays while leopard sharks linger around Shark Point on calm days
Jul.conditions: monsoon swell and rain in bursts with outer-site trips weather-dependent

Aug.marine_life: turtles and morays holding on while schooling snapper stack the protected bays
Aug.conditions: rough windward seas and rain with some crossings cancelled and reduced visibility

Sep.marine_life: macro life thriving in the nutrient-rich water while marine life stays unchanged but harder to reach
Sep.conditions: wettest month with the biggest swell and most cancellations, sheltered sites only

Oct.marine_life: fish stocks fattened by monsoon nutrients as leopard sharks return to the pinnacles
Oct.conditions: monsoon tapering with seas settling late in the month and visibility improving

Nov.marine_life: first mantas returning to Racha Noi with leopard sharks and thick soft-coral pinnacles in full colour
Nov.conditions: dry season beginning with seas flattening quickly and visibility climbing

Dec.marine_life: mantas cruising in while leopard and blacktip sharks, turtles and barracuda tornadoes swirl at the Bidas
Dec.conditions: prime dry-season diving with calm crossings to every local site

destinations.highlights: Easy day-boat diving from a full-service island hub: leopard sharks and soft-coral pinnacles at Shark Point, the 85m King Cruiser wreck, manta chances at Racha Noi, and Phi Phi's limestone walls all within a day's reach.
destinations.currents: Tidal currents run north-south across the mid-channel pinnacles and can be brisk at Shark Point, Anemone Reef and the King Cruiser, especially around spring tides. The Racha Islands are gentler, though Racha Noi's exposed tips can pump when the manta action is best.
destinations.current_note: strongest at the mid-channel pinnacles and wreck on spring tides
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit year-round; water rarely drops below 28°C

dive_sites.name: Shark Point (Hin Mu Sang)
dive_sites.blurb: Three soft-coral-smothered limestone pinnacles famous for leopard sharks resting on the sand.

dive_sites.name: Anemone Reef (Hin Jom)
dive_sites.blurb: A submerged pinnacle carpeted in sea anemones that pulls in hunting trevally, tuna and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: King Cruiser Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An 85m car ferry sunk in 1997 after striking Anemone Reef, now thick with schooling snapper, lionfish and bamboo sharks.

dive_sites.name: Racha Noi South Tip
dive_sites.blurb: Granite boulders and a cleaning station with Phuket's most reliable oceanic manta encounters in season.

dive_sites.name: Racha Yai Bay 1
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered east-coast hard-coral slopes with reliably clear water, ideal for training and easy multi-dive days.

dive_sites.name: Koh Doc Mai
dive_sites.blurb: A limestone islet whose sheer wall of sea fans and oysters hides seahorses, ghost pipefish and other prized macro.

dive_sites.name: Koh Bida Nok (Phi Phi)
dive_sites.blurb: Phi Phi's signature limestone karst dive, mixing soft-coral walls and overhangs with blacktip reef sharks and turtles.

destinations.description: Phuket is Thailand's day-trip diving workhorse: big comfortable boats leave Chalong every morning for the granite boulders of the Racha Islands, the leopard-shark pinnacles of Shark Point and Anemone Reef, the King Cruiser wreck, and Phi Phi's karst walls. Warm, easy water and sheltered training bays suit new divers, while the wreck, currents and Racha Noi's manta-patrolled south tip give experienced divers real teeth. November to April delivers the calm seas and best visibility.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: smmohsinnaseem (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Santorini

Jan.marine_life: groupers, morays and octopus hunkering down in the lava rock
Jan.conditions: cool water with winter winds and swell and most operators closed

Feb.marine_life: octopus prowling quiet reefs alongside morays and nudibranchs
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with exposed caldera sites often blown out

Mar.marine_life: nudibranchs and crustaceans staying active in the cold clear water
Mar.conditions: cold and windy late winter with boats rarely running

Apr.marine_life: reef fish waking up with wrasse, groupers and early octopus activity
Apr.conditions: season opening with water still chilly and visibility sharpening

May.marine_life: groupers, morays and octopus patrolling the lava walls beside busy wrasse
May.conditions: settling seas and empty sites with cool water needing a thick suit

Jun.marine_life: anthias clouding the walls while barracuda school and octopus and nudibranchs cover everything
Jun.conditions: warm calm and glass-clear water inside the caldera

Jul.marine_life: barracuda and tuna passing offshore while glassfish pack the caverns
Jul.conditions: warmest calmest diving with meltemi gusts topside but the caldera sheltered

Aug.marine_life: barracuda, groupers and rays hunting through dense baitfish as fish activity swells
Aug.conditions: hottest water of the year with superb visibility but the busiest boats

Sep.marine_life: big groupers out in the open while barracuda hunt among pelagic visitors
Sep.conditions: warm sea with calming winds and thinning crowds through a prime month

Oct.marine_life: groupers, morays and octopus staying active as the water cools
Oct.conditions: quiet end of season with still-warm water and first autumn swells

Nov.marine_life: octopus and cuttlefish working the black sand as only resident reef life remains
Nov.conditions: operators winding down with increasing wind and swell

Dec.marine_life: sparse winter reef life sheltering deep in the volcanic rock
Dec.conditions: cool rough winter sea with diving effectively shut down

destinations.highlights: Diving inside a flooded volcanic caldera on sheer lava walls, the shallow Santa Maria wreck off Nea Kameni and glass-clear Aegean water with 30m-plus visibility.
destinations.currents: The Aegean is virtually tideless and the caldera is sheltered, so water movement is minimal to non-existent at most sites. Outer-coast walls can pick up light surface chop and mild drift when the summer meltemi blows.
destinations.current_note: essentially still water inside the caldera
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm or shorty in Jul-Aug; 5mm for most of the season; 7mm in May, Oct and winter

dive_sites.name: Santa Maria Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 35m passenger ship sunk off Nea Kameni in the 1970s, sitting shallow enough for any certified diver and thick with glassfish and morays.

dive_sites.name: Nea Kameni Volcanic Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Lava flows and black volcanic boulders around the caldera's active young islet, with a WWII boat and a deeper tug lying nearby.

dive_sites.name: Adiavati Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A reef inside the caldera rim — locally tied to the Atlantis legend — crawling with crustaceans, invertebrates, groupers and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Aspronisi (White Island) Walls
dive_sites.blurb: Sponge-encrusted lava walls on the caldera's western edge that start at snorkel depth and plunge past 40m into deep blue.

dive_sites.name: The Caves
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow volcanic caverns, canyons and swim-throughs on the south coast, lit by shafts of sun and packed with glassfish and octopus.

dive_sites.name: Perissa Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Maze-like volcanic rock formations off the black-sand beach, an easy classroom reef full of wrasse, morays and crustaceans.

destinations.description: Santorini is diving inside a drowned volcano: sheer lava walls plunge from the caldera rim into deep cobalt water, and the shallow Santa Maria wreck rests off the still-steaming islet of Nea Kameni. Life is classic Mediterranean — groupers, morays, octopus, passing barracuda — sparse but sharply lit by 30m visibility and near-zero current. Best for divers who value geology, wrecks and effortless conditions over fish density, from easy shore reefs to deep caldera walls.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Andreas Pantziarides (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Tenerife

Jan.marine_life: angel sharks lying on the sand while stingrays gather and turtles cruise El Puertito
Jan.conditions: coolest water but crisp winter visibility with the south coast staying sheltered

Feb.marine_life: angel sharks encountered often while rays pair up in the caves beside morays and octopus
Feb.conditions: cool and clear with occasional Atlantic swell on exposed corners

Mar.marine_life: last reliable angel sharks lingering while stingrays, cuttlefish and trumpetfish are everywhere
Mar.conditions: clear late-winter water with seas settling as spring arrives

Apr.marine_life: angel sharks thinning out while turtles, barracuda schools and reef fish carry the show
Apr.conditions: transitional water still cool but generally calm with good visibility

May.marine_life: turtles about while bull rays cruise the drop-offs beside big schools of bream and roncadores
May.conditions: calm spring seas with warming water and reliably clear conditions

Jun.marine_life: rays staying active while turtles show daily at El Puertito with barracuda and bastard grunt schools
Jun.conditions: warm settled early summer with trade winds picking up above water

Jul.marine_life: turtles and stingrays about while trumpetfish cloud up and juveniles fill the volcanic reef
Jul.conditions: warm and busy with brief upwellings trimming visibility on some days

Aug.marine_life: stingray aggregations building off Las Galletas with turtles, octopus and cuttlefish about
Aug.conditions: warmest air and lively resorts with calm sea and occasional plankton haze

Sep.marine_life: bull rays and big barracuda cruising warm water while turtles and dense fish schools gather
Sep.conditions: sea at its warmest and calmest with long settled spells

Oct.marine_life: rays and turtles everywhere while roncador schools pack thick over the wrecks
Oct.conditions: warm water lingering with glassy autumn seas and excellent visibility

Nov.marine_life: stingrays massing while turtles roam and the first angel sharks edge into the shallows
Nov.conditions: still-warm water with quiet sites and clear autumn conditions

Dec.marine_life: angel sharks moving back in while rays, turtles and cuttlefish work the sand flats
Dec.conditions: mild winter with sheltered south coast diving and visibility sharpening

destinations.highlights: Volcanic Atlantic diving off Europe's doorstep — green turtles over the El Puertito seagrass, stingray aggregations off Las Galletas, the basalt-column Condesito wreck, and one of the world's few reliable winter windows on critically endangered angel sharks.
destinations.currents: The leeward south coast is well sheltered from the prevailing northeast trades, so most sites see little to no current. Exposed headlands and offshore banks can pick up moderate flow when Atlantic swell or spring tides run.
destinations.current_note: Mostly calm on the sheltered south coast
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full year-round; add a hooded vest or 7mm Dec-Apr, 3mm fine Aug-Oct

dive_sites.name: El Puertito
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered sandy bay near Armeñime where resident green turtles graze the seagrass at snoozing-pace depths.

dive_sites.name: El Condesito
dive_sites.blurb: Cement carrier lost in 1971, its scattered hull draped against hexagonal basalt columns and patrolled by trumpetfish and morays.

dive_sites.name: Los Chuchos
dive_sites.blurb: The 'city of stingrays' minutes off Las Galletas harbour, with overhangs, a small sunken fishing boat and the island's densest ray population.

dive_sites.name: Montaña Amarilla
dive_sites.blurb: Shore-access lunar landscape of yellow volcanic tuff with terraces, swim-throughs and winter angel shark sightings.

dive_sites.name: Tabaiba Wreck (El Peñón)
dive_sites.blurb: Intact 35m tug scuttled off Tabaiba, upright at 30m with engine-room penetration for advanced divers.

dive_sites.name: Palm-Mar Cave
dive_sites.blurb: Galleried lava cave below a 17m plateau where Atlantic stingrays gather to court in winter.

destinations.description: Tenerife turns volcanic geology into a year-round Atlantic playground: basalt columns, lava arches and yellow tuff terraces in water that never leaves the high teens. Green turtles hold court at El Puertito, stingrays mass off Las Galletas, and from December to March the sand hides critically endangered angel sharks — one of the few places on earth you can count on them. Easy shore entries and short boat rides suit new divers; wrecks and lava caves keep experienced hands busy.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tuxyso (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Zanzibar

Jan.marine_life: whale sharks passing offshore while turtles pack Mnemba and dolphins work the channel
Jan.conditions: kaskazi monsoon bringing warm calm seas and superb visibility

Feb.marine_life: reef fish forming spawning aggregations while green turtles and resident bottlenose dolphins patrol
Feb.conditions: hot and settled with glassy mornings and top-of-year visibility

Mar.marine_life: turtles and Napoleon wrasse holding while late-season whale sharks linger before the rains
Mar.conditions: calmest seas of the year as kaskazi fades with the warmest water

Apr.marine_life: reef life carrying on unchanged while runoff and plankton limit the sightings
Apr.conditions: masika long rains with heavy downpours, reduced visibility and some boats pausing

May.marine_life: macro life rewarding those who go while the big animals stay scarce
May.conditions: tail end of the long rains with choppy seas and patchy visibility

Jun.marine_life: healthy reef action returning while turtles and schooling trevally gather at Leven Bank
Jun.conditions: dry season beginning with a kusi breeze and cooling clearing water

Jul.marine_life: pelagics working the northern banks while dense reef fish cover Mnemba
Jul.conditions: kusi monsoon wind with some chop on crossings but good visibility

Aug.marine_life: first humpback whales migrating past Leven Bank and the Mnemba channel
Aug.conditions: breezy kusi winds with coolest water and reliable visibility

Sep.marine_life: humpback whales gathering in numbers while barracuda and tuna school offshore
Sep.conditions: winds easing with dry clear weather and excellent visibility

Oct.marine_life: late humpbacks overlapping with the first whale sharks while turtles roam everywhere
Oct.conditions: calm inter-monsoon lull with flat seas and superb visibility

Nov.marine_life: whale sharks building in numbers while dolphins and rays fill the channels
Nov.conditions: short vuli rains bringing brief showers with seas mostly workable

Dec.marine_life: whale sharks showing most frequently while nesting turtles stay active at Mnemba
Dec.conditions: kaskazi setting in with warm water and visibility climbing again

destinations.highlights: Mnemba Atoll's turtle-packed coral slopes and the current-swept pelagic seamount of Leven Bank, with humpback whales passing in September and whale sharks from October to February.
destinations.currents: Tides of up to five metres drive currents that range from slack to brisk on the Mnemba and Tumbatu reefs, making gentle drifts the norm. Leven Bank in the open Pemba Channel is another matter, with strong, unpredictable current that demands drift experience.
destinations.current_note: gentle reef drifts inshore; strong and shifty at Leven Bank
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit Nov-Apr; 3mm-5mm full in the cooler Jun-Sep season

dive_sites.name: Kichwani
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's classic sloping wall drift, dense with hard and soft coral, trevally, morays and well-camouflaged leaf fish.

dive_sites.name: Wattabomi
dive_sites.blurb: A gentle Mnemba slope of bommies and plate coral where green turtles are near-guaranteed and dolphins sometimes cruise through mid-dive.

dive_sites.name: Big Wall
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's vertical face of overhangs and sponge-crusted ledges, drawing tuna, barracuda, rays and the occasional hammerhead off the blue.

dive_sites.name: Leven Bank
dive_sites.blurb: An open-ocean seamount in the Pemba Channel where strong current pulls in kingfish, tuna, big morays and schooling barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Hunga Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A circular open-water reef off Nungwi known for huge coral bommies and consistently sharp visibility.

dive_sites.name: Tumbatu Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: Quiet, current-brushed gardens off Tumbatu Island regarded as Zanzibar's healthiest coral, with seahorses and turtles among the highlights.

destinations.description: Zanzibar pairs easy tropical reef diving with a genuine big-animal calendar. Day boats work Mnemba Atoll's coral slopes, where green turtles and dolphins are routine, while Leven Bank's current-raked seamount gives experienced divers kingfish, tuna and barracuda in the Pemba Channel. Two monsoons shape the year: kaskazi brings warm, glassy water from December to March, kusi cooler humpback-season seas from June to October, and the April-May long rains close the window. Suits new divers and drift-hungry veterans alike.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: I've Got It On Film! (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Dahab

Jan.marine_life: resident reef fish, morays and blue-spotted rays; quiet, fishy reefs
Jan.conditions: coolest water, occasional wind chop at exposed entries, clear Gulf of Aqaba viz

Feb.marine_life: healthy coral gardens, octopus and nudibranchs for slow macro dives
Feb.conditions: cold-for-the-Red-Sea water, breezy days, otherwise calm shore entries

Mar.marine_life: reef life waking up; garden eels, lionfish and schooling anthias
Mar.conditions: water at its coolest, wind easing, viz staying reliably clear

Apr.marine_life: anthias clouds thicken, turtles grazing the seagrass, first pelagics passing
Apr.conditions: warming fast, flat mornings, comfortable multi-dive days

May.marine_life: busy reefs, unicornfish and fusilier schools, frogfish finds for sharp eyes
May.conditions: warm, calm and settled — classic easy Dahab shore diving

Jun.marine_life: full-summer reef activity, jacks and barracuda off the walls, turtles common
Jun.conditions: flat glassy water, long days, excellent visibility

Jul.marine_life: pelagics closer inshore, dolphins occasionally cruise the Blue Hole
Jul.conditions: hot topside, warm calm sea, viz climbing toward its best

Aug.marine_life: warmest water brings dense fish life and the odd reef shark sighting
Aug.conditions: very hot on land, dead-calm water, peak 30m-plus visibility days

Sep.marine_life: reefs at their liveliest — trevally hunts, turtles, morays out in the open
Sep.conditions: warm water lingering, calm seas, superb visibility

Oct.marine_life: abundant reef fish, rays on the sand, great macro on the house reefs
Oct.conditions: ideal balance of warm water, mild air and clear, settled seas

Nov.marine_life: reefs still busy; octopus, seahorses in the seagrass, schooling snapper
Nov.conditions: cooling gently, mostly calm with occasional breezy afternoons

Dec.marine_life: resident reef community, uncrowded sites, relaxed critter hunting
Dec.conditions: cooler water and windier spells, but shore diving continues year-round

destinations.highlights: Walk-in Gulf of Aqaba diving at its most laid-back — the Blue Hole via El Bells, the Canyon's cathedral cracks, Lighthouse house-reef ease and a deep technical scene, all from the beach.
destinations.currents: The Gulf of Aqaba is sheltered and tide-weak, so most Dahab sites sit in still or gently drifting water. Wind can add surface chop and a mild longshore push at exposed entries like the Blue Hole, but true current is rare.
destinations.current_note: calm, near-currentless shore diving most days
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm in high summer (Jul-Sep); 5mm spring and autumn; 5-7mm with hood Dec-Mar

dive_sites.name: Blue Hole & El Bells
dive_sites.blurb: The classic drop down the Bells chimney, along the outer wall and over the 7m saddle into the world's most famous sinkhole.

dive_sites.name: The Arch (Blue Hole)
dive_sites.blurb: The infamous tunnel through the Blue Hole's wall at 55m — a serious, training-and-trimix-only technical dive.

dive_sites.name: The Canyon
dive_sites.blurb: A narrow crack in the reef plate that swallows divers into a fishbowl cavern lit by shafts of blue light.

dive_sites.name: Lighthouse Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Dahab's house reef in the middle of town — easy sloping coral, resident frogfish and seahorses, and the go-to night dive.

dive_sites.name: Eel Garden
dive_sites.blurb: A sandy amphitheatre where hundreds of garden eels sway in formation below a pretty coral entrance channel.

dive_sites.name: The Islands
dive_sites.blurb: A maze of hard-coral pinnacles and swim-through lagoons south of town — arguably Dahab's prettiest coral dive.

dive_sites.name: Gabr el Bint
dive_sites.blurb: A remote fan-coral wall reached by boat or camel along the coast, rewarding the trek with pristine gorgonians.

destinations.description: Dahab is the Red Sea at its most relaxed: a small Sinai town on the Gulf of Aqaba where nearly everything is dived from the beach. Gear up, wade in, and you're on the Canyon's glowing cavern, Lighthouse's critter-rich house reef or the Blue Hole via the Bells. Water stays calm and clear most of the year, winters run cooler, and a serious technical community pushes the deep lines. Ideal for low-stress repetitive diving, training and long stays.

destinations.name: Hurghada & El Gouna

Jan.marine_life: resident reef fish, moray eels, blue-spotted rays; quiet reefs and occasional dolphins in the lagoons
Jan.conditions: coolest water, frequent northerly wind and choppy crossings; sheltered sites stay diveable and shallow viz can be superb

Feb.marine_life: reef fish dense on nearshore reefs, Napoleon wrasse, big morays; little pelagic action
Feb.conditions: coldest month at 21-22C, windy with surface chop; outer reefs like Careless often blown out

Mar.marine_life: reefs waking up, spinner dolphins returning to Shaab El Erg, eagle rays passing the walls
Mar.conditions: water warming, winds easing but still gusty spells; clear water on calm days

Apr.marine_life: spinner dolphins reliable, turtles, schooling snapper and fusiliers thickening on the pinnacles
Apr.conditions: calm, predictable seas and light wind; comfortable crossings to Abu Nuhas

May.marine_life: dolphins, grey reef and whitetip sharks patrolling Careless Reef, big jacks and barracuda
May.conditions: flat seas, 25-26C water, excellent light; prime window for the wreck runs

Jun.marine_life: reef sharks at the offshore pinnacles, tuna and trevally hunting, turtles nesting season
Jun.conditions: warm settled summer sea, gentle breeze; long days ideal for two-tank wreck trips

Jul.marine_life: peak fish density on the reefs, occasional hammerhead in the blue off Careless and Umm Gamar
Jul.conditions: hot topside, 28C water and mostly calm seas; some afternoon wind chop

Aug.marine_life: everything on the reef plus pelagics in deeper water; mild plankton haze feeds the food chain
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year at 28-29C; hot deck, easy diving, slightly softer viz

Sep.marine_life: sharks and jacks still active, dolphins daily, reef life at its densest after summer
Sep.conditions: seas settle again, water still 28C, crowds thin; superb all-round conditions

Oct.marine_life: predators drawn in by baitfish, spinner dolphin pods, eagle rays over the plateaus
Oct.conditions: calm seas, 27C water and some of the clearest viz of the year

Nov.marine_life: dolphins, Napoleon wrasse, hunting trevally; last of the warm-water pelagic action
Nov.conditions: still 25C and mostly calm early month; wind builds late November

Dec.marine_life: reef life steady, dolphins sheltering in the lagoons; pelagics scarce
Dec.conditions: cooling to 23C with windy days and winter chop; site choice shifts to sheltered reefs

destinations.highlights: Easy warm-water reef diving out of a big dive town, with the Giftun Island coral gardens, the four-wreck graveyard of Abu Nuhas and shark-patrolled offshore pinnacles all within day-boat range.
destinations.currents: Most Giftun-area reefs see gentle to moderate currents that suit relaxed drift dives. Exposed offshore pinnacles like Careless Reef and the northern tips of Umm Gamar can run stronger when the wind is up.
destinations.current_note: mild on inshore reefs, stronger at exposed pinnacles
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full in summer (Jun-Oct); 5mm in spring and autumn; 5-7mm with hood for 21-23C winter water (Dec-Mar)

dive_sites.name: Giannis D
dive_sites.blurb: Tilted Greek freighter on Abu Nuhas with an intact stern and a glassfish-filled engine room that makes one of the Red Sea's best easy penetrations.

dive_sites.name: Carnatic
dive_sites.blurb: An 1869 steam-sail ship whose exposed ribs are now sheathed in soft coral, the most atmospheric of the Abu Nuhas graveyard.

dive_sites.name: Chrisoula K
dive_sites.blurb: The 'tile wreck' of Abu Nuhas, a cargo ship still stacked with Italian floor tiles and riddled with swim-throughs.

dive_sites.name: Careless Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Twin pinnacles rising from open water an hour offshore, the area's most reliable spot for grey reef sharks and the odd hammerhead in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Umm Gamar
dive_sites.blurb: Crescent island with a plateau of coral towers, a cavern swim-through at 28m and eagle rays cruising the drop-off.

dive_sites.name: Shaab El Erg (Dolphin House)
dive_sites.blurb: Horseshoe reef between Hurghada and El Gouna whose sheltered lagoon is a daytime rest stop for resident spinner dolphins.

dive_sites.name: Small Giftun
dive_sites.blurb: Classic drift along a soft-coral wall on Giftun Island's east side, dense with fan corals, turtles and passing eagle rays.

destinations.description: Hurghada is the Red Sea's workhorse dive hub, and it earns the traffic: warm water all year, 20-30m visibility, and day boats fanning out to the Giftun reefs, dolphin lagoons and the four-wreck graveyard at Abu Nuhas. Sheltered coral gardens make it a natural place to learn, while the Carnatic and Giannis D give wreck divers real substance. Winter brings wind and chop, so spring and autumn are the sweet spots.

destinations.name: Marsa Alam
destinations.difficulty: beginner-friendly bays to advanced offshore walls

Jan.marine_life: Dugong and green turtles in the seagrass bays; hammerheads gathering at Daedalus for liveaboards
Jan.conditions: Coolest water, windy with chop on offshore crossings; inshore viz at its annual peak

Feb.marine_life: Turtles and dugong inshore; big pelagic chances offshore for hardy divers
Feb.conditions: Cool 22-23C water and brisk north winds; crystal winter visibility

Mar.marine_life: Reef life waking up; turtles, napoleons and early manta sightings on outer reefs
Mar.conditions: Winds easing, water still cool; long clear sightlines

Apr.marine_life: Spring pelagic pulse — oceanic whitetip and hammerhead encounters pick up offshore
Apr.conditions: Warming, calmer seas open up Elphinstone and the far south

May.marine_life: Hammerheads schooling at Daedalus; spinner dolphins resident at Sataya and Samadai
